coredata.dc:title: Thermal management of Li-ion batteries: Experimentally validated model of active temperature control strategies with liquid cooling and multi-objective optimization
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Capasso, Clemente
Castiglione, Teresa
Perrone, Diego
Sequino, Luigi
coredata.dc:description: In the last decade, electric vehicles (EVs) have gained considerable attention in the transport sector as they represent a valid solution for reducing CO2 emissions in the short term, contributing to the achievement of net zero emissions by 2050. Despite the technology of EVs has reached a good level of reliability, challenges related mainly to the Li-ion storage systems and to their thermal management, are still open. A properly designed battery thermal management system (BTMS) is intended to maintain the lithium battery within its optimal temperature range to preserve its cycle-life durability, performance, and safety. Research efforts are, therefore, addressed to improve and optimize thermal management solutions for vehicle battery packs. In this work, an active indirect liquid BTMS for a Li-ion cell is designed and developed with the main goal of satisfying the thermal requirements of the storage cell, by also focusing on an optimal balance between BTMS performance and overall system efficiency. In particular, a laboratory test bench was designed and set-up to allow experimental tests focused on parameter identification/validation of an electro-thermal simulation model of the cell and on its temperature management. Experimental tests and simulations were performed for different environmental temperatures and electric operating conditions. In particular, the proposed BTMS was analyzed during both cooling and heating operations showing a good behavior in managing the coolant flow rate to achieve the temperature set-point. Starting from the validated simulation model, an off-line multi-objective optimization procedure was carried out to properly set up the temperature control parameters on real driving conditions. The obtained results show a good level of reliability of the proposed simulation models in fitting the electro-thermal behavior of the cell under tests in different operative conditions. In addition, the BTMS can properly manage the coolant flow rate to achieve the temperature set-points. Finally, the proposed optimization procedure allows a relevant reduction in the pump energy consumption in comparison with non-optimized solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Li-ion battery
Thermal management
Battery temperature optimal control

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid fundingbodyid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight oauserlicense openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder sponsoredaccesstype srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content oa subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst nomenclature primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental tests for model and controller validation
Multi-objective optimization procedure of thermal management controller parameters

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

Governo Italiano Ministero dell'Istruzione dell'Universita e della Ricerca

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research has been partially supported by the European Union-NextGenerationEU - National Sustainable Mobility Center CN00000023, Italian Ministry of University and Research Decree n. 1033\u201417/06/2022, Spoke 12, CUP B43C22000440001. Authors warmly acknowledge Prof. Sergio Bova, Dr. Bianca Maria Vaglieco, and Dr. Ottorino Veneri for their help in discussions and comments about the contents of this paper. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Daria La Gamba, Domenico Rombol\u00E0, and Andrea Sgr\u00F2 for their support during their internship periods for the Master's Degree theses.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tested Li-ion battery cell.
2D and 3D drawings of the channel plate and installation of the metal cover.
Scheme of the electro-thermal test bench for battery analysis.
Functional scheme of the temperature control system.
Functional scheme of the flow rate control.
Functional scheme of the control strategy.
Equivalent circuit model with an open circuit voltage source, an ohmic resistance with n RC pairs in series.
Heat exchange between the cell and the environment.
Block diagram of the developed cell thermal management system.
SOC, Voltage, and Current during the MST cycle.
Estimated parameters of the cell from the MST experiments.
Battery Voltage (a) and temperature (b) for 1C discharge tests with no cooling.
Battery Voltage (a) and temperature (b) for 2C discharge tests with no cooling.
Battery Voltage (a) and temperature (b) for 2C discharge tests when plate channels are filled with still water.
Battery Voltage (a) and temperature (b) for 2C discharge tests when a coolant flow rate of 0.55 l/min is enforced.
Comparison between numerical and experimental variables for Test#1.
Comparison between numerical and experimental variables for Test#2.
Comparison between numerical and experimental variables for Test#3.
Comparison between numerical and experimental variables for Test#4.
Current input to the dynamic model both for 1C and 2C discharge rates.
Battery current (a) and environmental temperature (b) profiles for the optimization procedure.
Workflow of the proposed multi-objective optimization procedure.
Response surfaces for maximum values of pump electric energy consumption (a) and temperature error (b).
Contour plots for non-dominated solutions.
Comparison between optimized and non-optimized thermal management parameters for the considered driving cycle.
Comparison between optimized and non-optimized thermal management parameters for 2C constant current discharging test.
Cell specifications.
Thermocouple specifications.
Values adopted for the estimation of the global heat transfer coefficient.
Experimental test matrix for cooling and heating.

head.ce:title: Thermal management of Li-ion batteries: Experimentally validated model of active temperature control strategies with liquid cooling and multi-objective optimization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Sciences and Technologies for Sustainable Energy and Mobility, National Research Council of Italy, Via G. Marconi, 4, 80125 Naples, NA, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Sciences and Technologies for Sustainable Energy and Mobility

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Sciences and Technologies for Sustainable Energy and Mobility, National Research Council of Italy, Via G. Marconi, 4, 80125 Naples (NA), Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical, Energy and Management Engineering, University of Calabria, Ponte Bucci, Cubo 46C, 87036 Rende, CS, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical, Energy and Management Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical, Energy and Management Engineering, University of Calabria, Ponte Bucci, Cubo 46C, 87036 Rende (CS), Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the last decade, electric vehicles (EVs) have gained considerable attention in the transport sector as they represent a valid solution for reducing CO2 emissions in the short term, contributing to the achievement of net zero emissions by 2050. Despite the technology of EVs has reached a good level of reliability, challenges related mainly to the Li-ion storage systems and to their thermal management, are still open. A properly designed battery thermal management system (BTMS) is intended to maintain the lithium battery within its optimal temperature range to preserve its cycle-life durability, performance, and safety. Research efforts are, therefore, addressed to improve and optimize thermal management solutions for vehicle battery packs.

In this work, an active indirect liquid BTMS for a Li-ion cell is designed and developed with the main goal of satisfying the thermal requirements of the storage cell, by also focusing on an optimal balance between BTMS performance and overall system efficiency. In particular, a laboratory test bench was designed and set-up to allow experimental tests focused on parameter identification/validation of an electro-thermal simulation model of the cell and on its temperature management.

Experimental tests and simulations were performed for different environmental temperatures and electric operating conditions. In particular, the proposed BTMS was analyzed during both cooling and heating operations showing a good behavior in managing the coolant flow rate to achieve the temperature set-point. Starting from the validated simulation model, an off-line multi-objective optimization procedure was carried out to properly set up the temperature control parameters on real driving conditions.

The obtained results show a good level of reliability of the proposed simulation models in fitting the electro-thermal behavior of the cell under tests in different operative conditions. In addition, the BTMS can properly manage the coolant flow rate to achieve the temperature set-points. Finally, the proposed optimization procedure allows a relevant reduction in the pump energy consumption in comparison with non-optimized solutions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: surface area of one side of the cell [m2]
heat transfer coefficient from plate side [W/m2K]

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, energy and transportation sectors are facing important transition phases, pushed by growing global concerns about climate change and anthropogenic emissions. In particular, according to the evaluations of the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change [1], mandatory mitigation actions are required to avoid an expected increase of 2.5 °C in the global average temperature by 2050. At the same time, due to general improvements in economic levels, especially for emerging countries, the global demand for energy sources, with particular reference to liquid fossil fuels, is still rising at an alarming rate [2]. In this regard, new policies and actions have been proposed by different countries with the main aim of reaching carbon neutrality for a more sustainable future. As an example, the Fit For 55 plan proposed by the European Commission, sets the target for European Countries to reduce net greenhouse gas emissions by at least 55 % by 2030 and become climate neutral by 2050 [3]. To deal with the above issues, increasing attention has been focused on developing high-efficiency road vehicles, based on electric propulsion systems, and their optimal integration with renewable energy sources supported by a smart grid scenario. As evaluated in [4], the use and clean integration of these kinds of vehicles could lead to a reduction of about 40 % in greenhouse gas emissions related to the private transportation sector.

Starting from the above context, in recent years the diffusion in the global automotive market of electric vehicles (EVs) has been characterized by a rapid and constant increase. This diffusion started from the corporate and car-sharing sectors, mainly because of EVs' low performance in terms of driving range and charging times, which initially discouraged a great part of private users. In the last decades, the EV market has been boosted by the wide adoption of recent lithium-based storage technologies. Indeed, these technologies are characterized by high energy/power density values and an acceptable cost, which, considering large-scale production, could make EVs almost comparable with traditional vehicles [5]. Consequently, new EVs' performance can now be considered attractive for private users, with driving ranges up to about 600 km and complete charging time reduced to <1 h [6]. On the other hand, EVs can still be considered a new vehicle technology with many open challenges to be properly addressed by researchers and manufacturers. These challenges are mainly related to Li-ion storage systems and their thermal management since their operative temperature has recognized negative effects on calendar life, safety concerns, and performance degradation [7]. As a matter of fact, the acceptable temperature range for lithium batteries generally ranges from −20 °C to 60°C, whereas optimal performance can be obtained in the narrow range 15°C–35°C [8]. Temperature values higher than the acceptable range can result in triggering a thermal runaway process and explosion, with related issues in terms of safety [9]. In the same way, operative temperatures outside the narrow optimal range have recognized negative effects on performance degradation and life reduction [10].

The above considerations justify the increasing research interest in optimal thermal management solutions for vehicle battery packs. In particular, the classification of Battery Thermal Management Systems (BTMSs) is generally based on the heat transfer mediums, and they are identified as air, liquid, or PCM-based BTMSs [11]. Each technology has its advantages and drawbacks, which should be properly considered in the design and sizing of the overall vehicle battery pack. At the present state of technology, forced air and liquid-based BTMS are generally most used for road vehicle applications. The main advantages of forced air BTMS generally consist in low maintenance and installation costs, low space requirements, and the absence of possible liquid leakages. On the other hand, these systems are characterized by different drawbacks, which are mainly related to the low heat transfer coefficient of the air, the dependency on the ambient temperature, the energy required by the fan, and the non-uniform temperature distribution [12]. Better performance can be surely obtained with liquid-based BTMS, although they are characterized by higher complexity and cost. In particular, a detailed comparison between air and liquid technologies is reported in [13] for the case study of a prismatic high-energy lithium-ion 48 V battery pack. In this case, the obtained results highlight the better performance of liquid BTMS in terms of temperature uniformity among the cells and overall system efficiency. A detailed comparison between direct and indirect liquid BTMS is reported in [14]. In the first case, the direct contact between the battery cells and the coolant enhances the thermal exchange process and simplifies the structure of the system. These systems can save space and cost but a higher energy consumption is required because of the high viscosity of the coolant. Although direct liquid BTMSs are considered a promising solution for the future, at the present state of technology, indirect systems represent the most used solution for EV applications mainly because of their great advantage in terms of maintenance operations [14,15]. In these cases, either pure water or water/glycol blend is generally used as a coolant, even though the use of fluids doped with nanoparticles (nanofluids) has shown promising results due to the increase in thermal conductivity [16,17]. Different studies have also focused on the design of cold plate channels, which can directly affect the thermal performance and energy consumption of indirect liquid BTMSs. In particular, geometrical optimization procedures are described in [18–20] for serpentine-channel cooling plates. The optimization procedures are carried out by varying channels' width, position, and layout in a simulation environment to optimize the overall performance and efficiency of the considered BTMS.

The last aspect that must be optimized for the battery temperature control is the cooling strategy which can be varied according to the operating conditions. In this regard, there are some recent studies about a non-linear model predictive control for the management of forced air BTMS, proposed in [21]. An intelligent battery temperature controller based on Fuzzy Logic Control (FLC) and Reinforcement Learning Control (RLC) is proposed and validated in a simulation environment in [22,23] for an indirect liquid-based BTMS. In both cases, simulation results highlight overall better temperature management performance and efficiency compared with PID-based control methods. Dynamic programming and a genetic algorithm are proposed in [24] to optimize the overall battery system performance and life. Recent researches also focus on the use of machine-learning approaches to support advanced BTMSs. In particular, these approaches are proposed for either thermal data prediction or system optimization [25]. In this regard, Guo et al. [26] propose a battery temperature prediction algorithm based on Long Short Term Memory - Neural Networks (LSTM - NNs) to support a BTMS for lithium-ion modules combining indirect liquid-cooling with forced air-cooling. Similarly, a Back Propagation Neural Network is considered in [27] for predicting the maximum cell temperature value and its gradient within the battery pack under analysis. In this case, the cooling action is performed through a hybrid phase change materials (PCMs)/forced air BTMS. Optimization procedures based on a Multi-layer Perceptron NN (MLP) are proposed in [28] for the optimal design of a liquid immersion cooling BTMS on the basis of three input parameters: battery cells' spacing, liquid inlet velocity, and number of inlets. The optimization is based on the comparison of the predicted outputs (i.e. maximum temperature, temperature differences, and pumping power consumption) for different combinations of the above input parameters. Data-driven machine learning (ML)-based surrogate models are proposed in [29], in combination with a physics based model, to optimize the structural design and control protocols of a liquid-cooling BTMS. The simulation results have highlighted increased performance in terms of system efficiency and maximum achieved temperature.

The above analysis shows a consistent number of scientific contributions related to the design of cold plates and temperature management and control for BTMSs. On the other hand, with particular reference to indirect liquid systems, a great part of the analyzed papers is based on evaluations carried out in numeric simulations and CFD environments, highlighting a lack of consolidated experimental knowledge on these kinds of systems operating under real conditions.

In this context, this paper proposes a comprehensive study related to design, simulation, experimental validation, and optimization of an active indirect liquid BTMS for the case study of a Li-Ion cell. The main novelty consists in the use of high-fidelity experimentally validated simulation models to support a multi-objective optimization procedure, based on genetic algorithms, for the optimal design and management of the considered BTMS in terms of efficacy and efficiency, under load and temperature conditions representative of real on-board vehicle behavior. The obtained experimental results highlight the good fitting performance of the models and the good behavior of the BTMSs in maintaining the cell temperature values in the desired operative range by minimizing, at the same time, the pump energy consumption.

The paper is organized as follows: Section 2 summarizes the methodology with particular focus on the case study, experimental set-up, modeling and control; Section 3 is focused on the discussion of the results both for the single storage cell and for the overall thermal management system; finally, Section 4 presents the conclusions with the main achievements and future works.

For the experimental campaign, a 4890 mAh - Li-ion battery cell was used. The cell has a wounded flat configuration with the current collectors of aluminum (positive) and copper (negative). The cell features a LiC6 anode and a LiMn2O4 cathode. The maximum, nominal, and cut-off voltage are 4.2 V, 3.8 V, and 2.8 V, respectively. The nominal power is 18.5 W. The cell specifications are listed in Table 1 and a figure of the cell under test is reported in Fig. 1 :

A channel plate for liquid cooling of the cell under investigation was designed on purpose and realized via 3D printing (Fig. 2 ). First, the channel plate was digitally designed with a Computer Aided Design (CAD) software. It consists of a serpentine channel with one inlet and one outlet placed on the same side. The flow is divided into two paths to improve the heat exchange without producing significant pressure drops. The 3D model was printed with the Bambo Lab X1-Carbon printer using PLA commercial material, the printing time is of about 2 h. The channel plate was sealed with silicone on a metal plate of 0.4 mm thickness, whose role is to enhance the heat dissipation from the cell surface to the cooling liquid flowing in the channels. Finally, the assembled components were fixed to the battery using a high conductivity paste with a thermal conductivity of about 3 W/mK.

It is important to highlight that the proposed system aims to allow experimental tests focused on the evaluation of heat exchange between the liquid, the metal cover, and the battery under investigation. The PLA support is just used for the considered laboratory application to easily obtain the desired liquid flows through the ducts. For reliable vehicle application, different kinds of materials should be used, based on the consideration of further aspects related to thermal stability and mechanical properties under long-term high and low temperature operations.
The cell was tested at the STEMS-CNR Laboratories and a schematic of the test bench is reported in Fig. 3 .

The test-rig is composed of a bi-directional controlled DC power supply unit, working in combination with an electronic DC load, which allows for carrying out electric characterization tests with current and voltage values up to 120 A–80 V under charging and discharging cycles. The proposed electric test bench can reproduce steady-state and dynamic charging/discharging cycles for the storage unit under test. An I/O analog module that can manage up to 8 K-type thermocouples is installed. K-type thermocouples, whose specifications are listed in Table 2 , were used to monitor the temperature in different locations of the system: one was installed on the channel plate surface, one between the plate and the cell, and one on the free, not cooled, side of the cell. Moreover, two more thermocouples were used to monitor the ambient and the cooling liquid temperature. Finally, the cell temperature control was performed through a climate chamber, which can be set to either fixed or variable temperatures in the range of −40 ÷ +180 °C.

All the temperatures and the relevant parameters, as battery voltage and current, were monitored and recorder using an acquisition system based on a National Instruments CDAQ 9188 and a software interface realized in the Labview® environment.

A variable mass flow rate pump with input voltage regulation was used to provide the variable water supply to the cooling plate. The mass flow rate variation was obtained by varying the pump speed, controlled by the applied voltage. The pump has a maximum voltage of 12 V, a delivery head of 6 m, and maximum mass flow rate of 13 l/min. Both hydraulic and electrical characterizations of the pump were carried out. The relationship between mass flow rate (
m c
̇
) versus input voltage (V) was measured and reported in Eq. (1); similarly, Eq. (2) represents the relationship between the pump power (P) and the delivered flow rate (
m c
̇
). A linear behavior, with voltage, can be observed for both cases.
(1)
m c
̇
= 0.247 ∗ V − 0.557
l min
(2)
P = 6.69 ∗
m c
̇
+ 0.43
W

From measurements, the minimum voltage for the pump activation was 4.2 V, which corresponds to a flow rate of 0.48 l/min. In addition, it was observed that by increasing the flow rate up to 0.65 l/min, the pump power absorption was of about 4.8 W that corresponds to 26 % of the pump nominal power. This value was selected as the pump upper limit for pump control in order not to use the battery power beyond 25 % of its capacity for cooling purposes.

The cell temperature control was performed through an external temperature management system, which activates and regulates the water flow rate as a function of the reference temperature and is based on an ARDUINO UNO board. The choice of this board is related to the fact that temperature variation is generally slow and therefore cheap systems can be conveniently used for these kinds of control. A schematic of the experimental cell temperature control is reported in Fig. 4 . The main board manages the activation and regulation, in speed/flow-rate, of an external water pump using an L298N H-Bridge module, which is directly controlled by the PWM signals coming from the board. Two water tanks were used as source and sink to operate with the cooling liquid at a constant temperature during the laboratory experimental tests.
The flow rate control of the pump is based on a proportional control scheme, which is reported in the figure below (Fig. 5 ):

It is important to highlight that the temperature control system operates as an active control, aiming to maintain the cell operations in optimal temperature conditions and reducing the effects of both high and low temperatures on its performance and remaining useful life. The output of the proportional gain is directly related to the duty cycle of the H-bridge PWM, controlling the pump voltage supply. In this case, a preliminary calibration of the pump mass flow rate as a function of the pump voltage supply was performed. The main scheme of the proposed control strategy is reported in the following figure (Fig. 6 ).

In particular, the cooling part is activated when the cell temperature overcomes a maximum temperature value, T
max , and the liquid temperature, T
cool , is lower than the cell temperature. In the same way, the heating part is activated when the cell temperature is lower than the threshold value, T
min , and the liquid temperature, T
cool , is higher than the cell temperature.

For what concerns the proper settings of the main parameters for the above temperature control strategies, in this paper, a multi-objective optimization procedure is proposed based on system modeling. In particular, the procedure is aimed at maximizing the cooling system efficiency and optimizing at the same time the accuracy of the proportional control system. Therefore, for the specific use case, the chosen objective functions are the total amount of energy required by the pump, E
p , and the maximum error, ε
max , as described in Figs. 5 and 6.

In order to implement the above described control, a model of the system, for real-time applications was developed. The cell thermal model was developed following a lumped-parameters approach, which is particularly suitable for control-oriented real-time applications. The model predicts the battery/cell temperature, voltage, and state of charge by receiving as model inputs the operating current or power, the coolant inlet temperature, the flow rate, and the ambient air temperature. The starting point for model development is given by the energy balance during charge/discharge phases. During both phases, in fact, the electrochemical reactions occurring inside the battery cause inner heat generation, which is partly lost to the environment (ambient air or coolant) and partly stored in the battery, so that the energy balance can be written as:
(3)
m cell
c
p cell
d
T cell
dt
=
Q ̇
gen
−
Q ̇
env
−
Q ̇
cool

The first term represents the cell internal energy rate, where
m cell
is the mass of the cell,
c
p cell
is the cell specific heat and
T cell
the cell surface temperature.
Q ̇
gen
is the internal heat generation and
Q ̇
env
and
Q ̇
cool
are the losses to the environment and the coolant, respectively.

In Eq. (3), the internal heat generation,
Q ̇
gen
, during charging/discharging phases is estimated by adopting an equivalent circuit model (ECM), which represents a more computationally efficient approach to modeling the dynamic behavior of a battery pack/cell than the electrochemical models [30,31]. In particular, the electrical behavior of the cell is represented by a circuit, as reported in Fig. 7 , in which the open circuit voltage, U
oc , represents the cell potential at rest; the cell working voltage, U
cell , however, is lowered by the presence of resistors (R
ohm ) and resistor-capacitor pairs (R
n , C
n ), which take into account the ohmic losses experienced at both electrodes and at the electrolyte and the losses due to polarization effects, respectively.

According to this approach, the internal heat generation, which includes both the resistive Joule heat and the reversible entropic heat of the cell during the charge/discharge process, is computed in terms of charge/discharge cell operating current, I (positive for discharge and negative for charge), open-circuit voltage and working voltage as:
(4)
Q ̇
gen
= I
U OC
−
U cell
−
d
U OC
d
T cell
T cell
where
U OC
is obtained for different SOC levels from an experimental campaign, as will be described in the next section;
U cell
is computed as:
(5)
U cell
=
U OC
−
R ohm
I −
U 1
−
U 2
− …
U n

U n
is obtained from the voltage across the RC pairs of the Thévenin equivalent circuit model, with the following differential equation:
(6)

U th

where

and
C th
are the resistance and capacitance of the RC pair, respectively, with th = 1..n. Similarly, as
U OC
, the resistance and capacitance of the Thévenin circuit will be determined experimentally as a function of temperature and state of charge. The reversible entropic heat,
d

d
T cell
, is obtained from data present in the literature [32] and implemented in the model via lookup tables, as a function of the SOC. The state of charge, in turn, can be computed as:
(7)

where C
nom is the nominal battery capacity in Ampere-hour (Ah) and SOC
0 is the initial state of charge.

The heat losses from the cell to the environment,
Q ̇
env
, are computed as:
(8)

where A is the cell heat exchange surface area of each side and h
air and h
plate are the convective heat transfer coefficients between the cell and the environment from cell side and plate side, respectively, when the cooling system is not activated and therefore, the plate channels are filled with still air or coolant, as displayed in Fig. 8 .

In Eq. (8), h
plate is obtained as:
(9)

being s and λ the thickness and thermal conductivity of each layer, respectively. In addition, the heat transfer coefficient is calculated both for the case in which cooling is not foreseen (air in the plate ducts), and in the case in which the channels are filled with stagnant coolant. Layers specifications are collected in Table 3 :

When the cooling system is activated,
Q ̇
env
is computed by considering only the first term in Eq. (8), the second term is neglected because on the plate side the heat is mainly dissipated to the liquid:
(10)
Q ̇
env

T cell
−
T env

With regard to the heat dissipated by the coolant flowing into the plate channels, included in Eq. (3) as
Q ̇
cool
, this is computed as:
(11)
Q ̇
cool
=
h cool
A
T cell
−
T cool
where the heat transfer coefficient between the cell and the coolant, h
cool , is determined with the following equation:
(12)
h cool
=
Nu
λ
L

In the above equation, L is the channel characteristic length amounting to 70 mm, λ is the thermal conductivity of the coolant (0.6 W/mK) and Nu is the Nusselt number for the flow on a flat plate, obtained as a function of Reynolds and Prandtl numbers (Re ≈ 1670, Pr ≈ 0.07). In the model, both ambient air and inlet coolant temperature,
T
cool , in
, are measured and given as model inputs.

The model also includes the controller described in Section 2.2, which, based on the computed
T cell
,
T env
, and the set-point value, s, computes the duty cycle, d, corresponding to the corrected coolant flow rate.
The schemes of the developed model and controller are reported in Fig. 9 .

The model requires an estimation of the equivalent circuit electrical parameters (
U OC
,
R ohm
,
R n
, and
C n
), in Eqs. (4), (5) and (6), as well as the estimation of the heat transfer coefficients in Eqs. (9) and (10).

For the first parametrization study, a multi-step test (MST) was performed on the cell. The test was carried out by enforcing a series of 6 discharge-resting phases, as displayed in Fig. 10 [37]. The discharge duration, at a constant current of 4.9 A, was set to 12 min at the beginning and the end of the test, and to 24 min for the intermediate steps. The resting phase duration was set to 30 min. The total duration of the test was about 4 h 43 min (17,000 s).

The identification of the main parameters for the Equivalent Circuit Model was carried out through an iterative error optimization process, based on the Matlab Parameter Estimation Tool, which compares experimental and simulation outputs for the same input current values. The considered cost function is the Sum Squared Error with a function tolerance set at 0.001. Nonlinear least square was used as optimization method with the trust-region-reflective algorithm [33]. The same procedure was repeated for two temperature values, 25 °C and 100 °C. The parameter extrapolated for each SOC value is reported in Fig. 11 :

These values were included in the model by means of look-up tables and, owing to the variation of the above parameters with temperature, their values were interpolated at each step of intermediate temperature to improve the model accuracy.

As for thermal parameters, and in particular for the heat transfer coefficients,
h air
and
h plate
, specific experimental campaigns were conducted to determine and validate their values. In particular, two discharge tests with different current values (1C and 2C discharge rates), were performed in the absence of the cooling system and with empty plate channels. The tests were performed starting from 100 % SOC, corresponding to a 4.2 V cell voltage. The current was kept constant at 4.9 A and 9.8 A for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively. The tests were stopped for a cut-off voltage of 2.8 V. The electro-thermal model was run in the same conditions using a first-attempt value of
h air
and computing
h plate
from Eq. (9) and was calibrated in order to achieve the best fit with the experimental data for both cases, which resulted for
h air
=20 W/m2K and
h plate
=5.73 W/m2K. The value of
h plate
is in line with the results of a previous study of the authors on the same cell, in natural convection, with variable current load [34]. For a sample of 8 discharge cycles, with C- rate varying from 0 to 2, the convective heat transfer coefficient in natural convection spanned from 2.5 to 4 W/m2K, which confirms the validity of the actual value where a different setup is used. For the tests carried out at 1C discharge rate, the highest errors amount to 0.27 V (8.4 %) and 2.6 °C (6.4 %), for voltage and temperature, respectively; for the case in which 2C discharge rate was applied, the errors amount to 0.13 V (4.5 %) for voltage and to 2.53 °C (4.6 %) for cell temperature. The results are reported in Figs. 12 and 13
.

Another experimental campaign was carried out in order to validate
h plate
, when the plate channels are filled with still water. In this case, a 2C discharge test was performed and the results achieved by adopting
h plate
=15.62 W/m2K are reported in Fig. 14 , with maximum errors of 0.19 V (5.5 %) and 2.51 °C (7.4 %), for voltage and temperature, respectively. The calculated value of h
plate is in line with previous studies from the authors on the same cell [35], relative to coatings of carbon, graphene, and silicon. In that case, the convective heat transfer coefficient spanned from 16 to 18 W/m2K at 1C discharge rate and from 14 to 20 W/m2K at 0.5C discharge rate. Also, the work highlighted that the higher the C-rate, the lower the uncertainty values. Also, in [36] a charging test with the CCCV method was tested and modeled, revealing that the best estimation of the convective coefficient was 26 W/m2K.

As a general behavior of the cell, it can be observed that, at the beginning of the test, when the discharge current is applied, a rapid reduction in the cell voltage, related to the inner cell resistance, is recorded. Subsequently, cell voltage reduction is almost linear when a constant discharge current is applied; finally, when the SOC is reduced to about 30 %, there is a rapid voltage decrease related to a further increase in cell resistance. As for cell temperature behavior, the rapid temperature rise at the beginning of the test is related to the low temperature difference between the cell and the environment. In this case, in fact, the internal heat generation and the low thermal exchange with the ambient air, favors the temperature rise. When the temperature difference between the cell and the environment is higher, the increased heat exchange makes the cell temperature rise slower. Finally, when the SOC reaches the 30 %, the higher inner resistance makes the temperature rise faster once again. When the cut-off voltage occurs, the cell temperature starts to decrease following a reverse-exponential profile up to reach the thermal equilibrium with the ambient.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental tests for model and controller validation
ce:section.ce:para: Further experiments were carried out with the main goal to validate the battery electro-thermal model when the battery thermal management is actuated. Repeated tests were performed for the battery being discharged at a constant current of 9.8 A, discharge rate 2C, starting from 100 % SOC, with an ambient temperature ranging between 25 and 26 °C, and a constant coolant flow rate of 0.55 l/min. The test was stopped for a cut-off voltage of 2.8 V.

Fig. 15 (a) and (b) shows the comparison between numerical and experimental data for battery voltage and cell temperature, respectively. A satisfactory agreement was achieved for the model output variables. The normalized root mean square error (NRMSE), defined as:
(13)
NRMSE = 100 ·
1
N i
∑
i = 1
N i
x EXP
−
x SIM
2
max
x EXP
− min
x exp
%
was used as a metric to define the model accuracy. The NRMSE values were 5.1 % and 7.6 %, for the voltage and the temperature curves, respectively. These differences, observed also for the previously analyzed cases, are mainly observed at the beginning and at the end of the test, where transient conditions occur. This difference is due to several factors. The most relevant one, which directly affects voltage estimation, is related to the adoption of the equivalent circuit model approximations, where the real chemical reactions at the electrodes and in the electrolyte and their time constants are not taken into consideration. These models are clearly simpler and less accurate than the ones based on the use of complex partial differential equations, which describe the electrochemical mechanisms inside the battery. More specifically, chemical reactions do not start instantaneously when the discharge current is applied as well as they continue for a short time after the discharge current is stopped. These effects are neglected in the estimation of the electric ECM parameters, so that voltage rapidly reduces when the discharge current is applied and rapidly increases when the test is stopped. The reliability of ECMs can be improved by increasing the number of RC pairs in the equivalent circuit, which, on the other hand, increase the computational effort. In this case, only one RC pair was considered as a good balance between the computational effort and the model fitting performance. As for cell temperature uncertainty, this is related to the variation of the electric model parameters with temperature, as reported in Fig. 11. For the present case, the simplified assumption that the values were linearly interpolated between 5 °C and 100 °C was adopted, while the real kinetic mechanisms vary with temperature in a quite complex way. From a sensitivity analysis, the parameter that mostly affects the temperature cell prediction is R0. While the experimental tests for the estimation of the electric parameters with temperature were carried out at T = 25 °C and 100 °C, a further model calibration aimed at determining the R0 values also at low temperature (T = 5 °C) was performed. Finally, another source of numerical error relies in the adoption of a lumped-parameters approach. This approach, in fact, predicts the average temperature value over the cell rather than the temperature gradients in one or two directions, while the comparison with the experimental data is made based on the data recorder by a thermocouple located at the cell center. Although the adopted assumptions represent a source of error, the deviations between the experimental data and model prediction are, however, small and the model accuracy can be considered acceptable for the specific real-time control-oriented application, as it will be further demonstrated in the next paragraphs.

Experimental tests were carried out in order to validate the controller for different environment conditions. To this aim, two environmental conditions were chosen: in one case, the external air temperature was set to 40 °C for which a proper cell cooling is requested; in the other case, the external temperature was set to 5 °C, for which, on the contrary, a proper cell heating is needed. The experiments are summarized in Table. 4 :

A comparison between the experimental data and the model results in terms of battery SOC, voltage, temperature, and coolant flow rate, for Test #1, is reported in Fig. 16 . The figure shows that the model predicts both electric and thermal variables with a high accuracy. In particular, for the voltage, the NRMSE amounts to 6.3 %, while for the temperature it is 3.3 %. Similarly, as for the previous case, the highest deviations occur during the transient phases at the beginning of the test and at the cut-off condition. As for the coolant flow rate control, the model predicts the corrected flow rate with a high accuracy level. In addition, the controller satisfies the control strategy described in Section 2.2, as it regulates the pump in order to fulfill the battery thermal requirements. In fact, it can be observed that starting from an environment temperature of 40 °C, by fixing a set point temperature of 20 °C for the cell and with a coolant temperature of 20 °C, the pump initially delivers the maximum coolant flow rate (about 0.64 l/min) in order to reach the set point value. The cell temperature reaches a steady condition after 110–120 s. When this condition is reached, the coolant flow rate is reduced to keep the temperature as close as possible to the set-point. However, with this constraint on maximum coolant flow rate, and with a coolant temperature of 20 °C, the set-point is not achieved, even though the cell temperature is considerably reduced from its initial condition.

In Test #2, the environmental conditions are the same as the previous case, but a 2C discharge rate is enforced. Fig. 17 shows that the response of the cell is very similar to the previous case and the NRMSE for voltage is about 9 %, while for the temperature it is about 5.8 %. The controlled coolant flow rate is quite well predicted also for this case. Focusing on the temperature, it can be observed that, similarly to the previous case, the pump initially delivers the maximum flow rate and when the cell temperature is close to the set-point, after about 120 s, the pump flow rate decreases in order to maintain the cell temperature closest to the set-point.

Results for cases Test#3 and Test#4, in which winter conditions are proposed with an external air temperature of 5 °C and a coolant temperature of 20 °C, which can be obtained by preheating the coolant, and characterized by discharge rates of 1C and 2C, are reported in Figs. 18 and 19
, respectively.

As seen for the previous cases, the highest differences between experimental data and numerical simulations are recorded during the transient phases for temperature and voltage, at the beginning of the tests and when the tests are stopped, while the State of charge and the controlled coolant flow rates have a quite good agreement. In particular, for Test#3 (Discharge rate 1C), the NRMSE amounts to 5.2 % and 3.1 % for voltage and temperature, respectively. As for coolant flow rate, the pump delivers its maximum flow rate at the beginning to warm-up the battery to the set-point value of 20 °C. After 250 s, the cell temperature reaches a steady value of 19 °C, which is close to the set-point, but, similarly to the cooling case, with the constraints on the maximum coolant flow rate delivered by the pump and the coolant temperature setting, the set-point is not achieved, even though a very close value is reached. For Test#4 (Discharge rate 2C), a slightly different behavior is observed. In this case, in fact, the set-point is exceeded, due to the internal heat generated by the high current value, which contributes to speed-up the heating process and increases the cell temperature. Hence, at the beginning, the pump delivers the maximum flow rate to speed-up the cell warm-up, but, when the set-point is achieved and the pump stops, the cell temperature continues to rise. However, also in this case, the model accuracy is demonstrated and the NRMSE, similarly to the previously considered cases, amounts to 4.1 % and 5.4 % for voltage and temperature, respectively.

For the above considered cases, it is also possible to observe that the dynamic response of the controller under transient conditions is comparable to a first-order system. The constant current discharge tests enforce a constant current step of 4.9 A and 9.8 A for 1C and 2C discharge rates, respectively (Fig. 20 ).

For 1C discharge rate, corresponding to a current step of 4.9 A, the dynamic response in terms of cell temperature (Figs. 16 and 18-Temperatures) reaches a constant value about 200 s after the step enforcement, both at the beginning of the discharging phase and when the test is stopped. Similarly, for the 2C discharge rate (current step of 9.8 A), the temperature response, displayed in Figs. 17 and 19, shows the achievement of a steady value after about 200 s from the application of the step, when the test begins. The time constant, τ, of the system can be calculated considering that 95 % of the steady value is reached at t = 3τ. It amounts in particular to τ = 41.6 s.

After the present discussion of experimental tests and simulations results, it can be concluded that, despite some inaccuracies related to the prediction of transient cell behavior, the model set up in this work is quite reliable for predicting the cell temperature, moreover, it properly satisfies the control strategy described in Section 2.2, allowing to reproduce its functioning, and then it can be considered a valid tool for the implementation of a more sophisticated multi-objective optimization procedure, aimed at maximizing the cooling system efficiency while optimizing the accuracy of the control system, as will be described in the next paragraph.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-objective optimization procedure of thermal management controller parameters
ce:section.ce:para: After the complete parametrization and experimental validation of the proposed storage cell simulation models under various environmental and operational conditions, with its temperature management system, a multi-objective optimization procedure is proposed to achieve optimal results, in terms of cooling system efficiency and temperature control system accuracy.

For this analysis, a specific use case was considered, based on real on-board measurements during vehicles' driving cycles reported in a previous paper by part of the authors. During this cycle, the cell under test, starting from a 60% SoC value, was discharged up to the 30 % of its SoC and then completely recharged at a constant current value of 3 A. At the same time, a linear environmental temperature variation was considered to explore a wide range of temperature values. This cycle was considered as representative of the storage cell on-board behavior, with a constant increase in the environmental temperature. The cell current and environment temperature profiles are reported in Fig. 21 .
Based on the above case study, the workflow of the considered multi-objective optimization problem, with the related parameters, constraints, and solution, is reported in Fig. 21.

As reported in the figure, multi-objective optimization problems involve the proper definition of decision variables, constraints, and objective functions to be optimized. In particular, decision variables represent the elements that can be tuned to address the objective functions' output. The constraints define the conditions to be satisfied by decision variables, optimal solutions, or both. Objective functions are the functions to be optimized by the considered procedure. A multi-objective problem is generally characterized by the simultaneous optimization of more than one of these functions, by finding trade-offs between conflicting objectives, respecting at the same time all the optimization constraints.

Concerning the first step related to variable definitions, on the basis of the results of experimental campaigns, the temperature value of 22.5 °C was chosen as an ideal operating point for the evaluations reported in this paper. Starting from this assumption, with reference to the control scheme reported in Fig. 6, T
max and T
min can be conveniently expressed by means of the equations (14).
(14)
T max
= 22.5 +
∆ T
2
T min
= 22.5 −
∆ T
2

Therefore, focusing on the control system accuracy, the term ΔT was chosen as the first decision variable for the optimization procedure. The second decision variable is represented by the desired temperature of the cooling liquid, T
cool .

For the optimization procedure related to the considered use case, the following constraints (16) were chosen for the decision variables:
T
cool , max
=
35 °
C
T
cool , min
=
15 °
C
(15)
∆
T max
=
15 °
C
∆
T min
=
1 °
C

As already mentioned in previous sections, the proper choice of objective functions aims to maximize the BTMS efficiency and its performance in controlling the cell temperature. Therefore, the total amount of energy required by the pump, E
p , and the maximum error of the temperature control system, εmax, were chosen as objective functions to be optimized.

To complete the problem definition reported in the above workflow, a polynomial approximation of the objective functions, for different values of decision variables, is derived through the methodology reported in [38,39], which consists of Design of Experiments (DoE), Surrogate Models (SMs).

The DoE deals with the systematic planning and execution of simulations to efficiently collect data related to the objective functions, while also considering the proper balance between computational effort and data reliability. For the DoE of this optimization problem, the Uniform Design (UD) approach was considered. This approach suggests the use of a uniformly distributed set of sample points within the decision variables' ranges defined by the constraints. The corresponding values of the objective functions are evaluated for each sample. Surrogate Models (SMs) are used to continuously approximate the system behavior in the complete range of the decision variables. This can be obtained through the use of the Response Surface Method (RSM) [40]. In particular, SMs were developed using data from simulations to define the objective functions in the context of the optimization problem. In RSM, by using the MATLAB curve-fitting tool, a mathematical model (usually a polynomial) is fitted to the collected data to approximate the response surfaces. These surfaces for the two objective functions are represented below in Fig. 23.

At this stage, the problem definition workflow reported in Fig. 22 is completed, and it can be properly solved through the use of genetic algorithms. Genetic algorithms are heuristic methods inspired by Darwin's theory, which suggests that organisms with advantageous traits are more likely to transmit those traits to future generations. In this case, the identification of advantageous traits is based on the associated value of the objective functions. As the genetic algorithm iterates, the organisms move toward the optimal solution. More details on the use and behavior of genetic algorithms can be found in [41]. In case of single-objective optimization procedures, the solution is unique, whereas in multi-objective optimization, the solutions form a set called the Pareto front. Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithms (MOGAs) have highlighted good performance in addressing these kinds of optimization challenges. In particular, they can be conveniently proposed for the optimization of real-time rule-based control strategies. Compared to other optimization techniques (i.e. Multi-Objective Particle Swarm Optimization), MOGAs can be time-demanding, due to a higher computational effort. On the other hand, they can reach better convergence, reducing the possibility of local optimal solutions [42]. These characteristics make MOGAs a better choice for off-line optimization procedures, which can be used for the proper tuning of thermal management systems. An example of these applications can be found in [43,44] for the case studies of fuel cell hybrid electric vehicles and hybrid thermal-electric leisure boats, respectively.

The implementation of MOGAs was performed by using the gamultiobj MATLAB function, with the parameters reported in the workflow of Fig. 22. The obtained contour plots for non-dominated solutions are reported in Fig. 24.

The operative point, which is highlighted in the yellow circles in Fig. 24(a) and (b), was chosen by considering the trade-off between the pump energy consumption and the temperature error, by giving higher priority to the first term, since it is directly related to the system efficiency. The results represented by this point are reported in the following.
∆ T =
10.35 °
C
T cool
=
20.60 °
C

The above solution related to variables' values involves the following results for the objective functions: Ep = 0.19 Wh, εmax = 7.54 °C. The comparison between the system behavior with and without the optimized parameters is reported in Fig. 25.

In particular, for the considered operative cycle a slight increase in the maximum cell temperature value (A) can be observed at the end of the cycle. This increase is considered acceptable since the storage cell continues operating within its optimal temperature range. On the other hand, it is clear that, thanks to the proposed optimization procedure, a significant decrease in the liquid pump energy consumption can be obtained (B), with a reduction in maximum value from about 3 Wh to 0.19 Wh. The slight increase in the maximum temperature error, reported in (C), can also be considered acceptable because of its very low effects on the cell temperature behavior.

As already mentioned, the above optimization was carried out for a driving cycle representative of real vehicle operations, which was obtained and scaled through on-board measurement campaigns. An additional case study has been considered to obtain further validation of the optimized controller when operating under demanding conditions. With this aim, a comparison between optimized and non-optimized solutions was performed, for the 2C discharging test reported in Fig. 17, with the environmental temperature profile linearly rising from 30 °C to 35°C. The obtained results are reported in Fig. 26.

Also for this case, the results highlighted the benefits of using the optimized controller. In particular, for the optimized solution, the battery cell was controlled to operate within the desired temperature range with a sensible reduction in the overall pump energy consumption (from 1.137 to 0.025 Wh evaluated at the end of the cycle).

The actual effects of the optimization procedure are even more evident by focusing attention on long-term battery aging processes. Indeed, battery aging is caused by calendar and cycling aging processes. In particular, the capacity loss related to cycling aging contribution can be evaluated on the basis of semi-empirical formulations reported in the scientific literature [45,46] on the basis of the following Eq. (16).
(16)
Q
cyc
loss , %
=
a ∗
T cell 2
+ b ∗
T cell
+ c
∗ exp
d ∗
T cell
+ e
∗
I rate
∗
Ah trhoughput
where a, b, c, d, e are specific coefficients directly related to the battery technology under test,
T cell
is the battery operative temperature, I
rate , is the battery current load normalized on the basis of its nominal capacity, Ah
throughput , represents the total amount of Ah stored/delivered by the battery over its lifetime. From the above equation, it is clear that the proper design and optimization of BTMS directly involves an attenuation in cycling capacity losses, due to optimal operative temperature levels for the battery pack and lower pump energy consumption. In particular, this last aspect involves a slight reduction in the I
rate term and a more sensible reduction in the Ah
throughput term, which takes into account the overall battery life.

Finally, the considered procedure is based on experimentally validated battery cell simulation models can involve relevant benefits in terms of the overall thermal management system efficiency and could be considered by researchers and manufacturers for optimal design procedures also for other technologies and applications of battery packs.

In this work, the thermal management of a Li-ion cell has been addressed combining different competencies to provide a complete and reliable tool for the road toward safe and efficient transport electrification. The investigation has been performed by modeling different hardware and software components of a battery thermal management system, including the cell electro-thermal model, the temperature control strategies, and the related hardware (electronics, water pump, channel plate). In addition, based on the validated simulation models a multi-objective procedure is proposed for the temperature controller optimization.

A laboratory test bench has been setup to perform experimental electric and thermal parametrization/validation procedures for the proposed simulation models. Moreover, the channel plate for the cell liquid-cooling has been designed and produced with 3d printing techniques. The test bench has been also equipped with an Arduino-based BTMS whose behavior and control parameters are also analyzed in simulation environment.

The results related to the single storage cell highlight the good agreement between simulated and experimental data. In this regard, the Normalized Root Mean Square Error (NRMSE) has been used as a model fitting performance index. In particular, for the complete system operating on a discharge test with a constant coolant flow of 0.55 l/min and a current of 9.8 A, the NRMSE of 7.6 % has been evaluated.

Different environmental temperature values have been set for the climatic chamber to reproduce the behavior of the temperature control strategy in summer and winter. Also, in these conditions a good accuracy of the model has been observed, resulting in a maximum NRMSE of 5.8 % for the temperature in summer conditions (cooling), and of 5.4 % in winter conditions (heating).

Finally, based on a real driving cycle record, the validated simulation model has been used in combination with a multi-objective optimization tool to reduce the pump energy demand and the difference between the actual and reference temperature of the cell under test. The optimization results in a sensible reduction of the overall pump energy consumption from 3 Wh to 0.19 Wh, maintaining the storage cell in its acceptable temperature range.

The outcomes of this research are also valuable for future developments, focusing on the optimization of the complete battery pack at vehicle-level. In this case, a proper structural design of the BTMS is defined, and the proposed approach can be adopted in order to achieve maximum efficiency for an EV when operating under real driving conditions and various environmental conditions.

Clemente Capasso: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Teresa Castiglione: Writing – original draft, Software, Conceptualization. Diego Perrone: Validation, Software. Luigi Sequino: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been partially supported by the European Union-NextGenerationEU - National Sustainable Mobility Center CN00000023, Italian Ministry of University and Research Decree n. 1033—17/06/2022, Spoke 12, CUP B43C22000440001. Authors warmly acknowledge Prof. Sergio Bova, Dr. Bianca Maria Vaglieco, and Dr. Ottorino Veneri for their help in discussions and comments about the contents of this paper. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Daria La Gamba, Domenico Rombolà, and Andrea Sgrò for their support during their internship periods for the Master's Degree theses.
